results.title: The Rise of Catherine the Great [DVD]
results.creator_credit: Paul Czinner, Alexander Korda, Ludovico Toeplitz, Korda-Toeplitz Productions and London Film Productions
results.brief_description: This biographical drama loosely based on historical facts is set in mid-18th century Russia during the reign of the somewhat domineering and determined Empress Elisabeth (played by the wonderful Dame Flora Robson) who in her waning years sets out to arrange a marriage between her nephew and heir to the throne, Grand Duke Peter (Douglas Fairbanks Jnr) and a young German princess named Catherine (Elizabeth Bergner). It is not long, however, before Catherine discovers that her dearly betrothed is both unfaithful and madly insane and yet for all this, she remains devoted and true to him. But with the death of Empress Elisabeth, and Peter’s ascension to the throne, life becomes more unpredictable and precarious for Catherine as Peter’s mental health and behaviour becomes more unstable. Circumstances take control and eventually Catherine is forced to assume power. This is an interesting piece of filming for its time and the quality acting and lavish sets certainly add to its depth. Cast also includes Gerald Du Maurier, Irene Vanbrugh, Diana Napier. NB: Filmed in black and white, the visual quality is quite dark in some scenes and the soundtrack is scratchy but still worth viewing.

results.title: Saving Grace
results.creator_credit: Nigel Cole, Mark Crowdy, Fine Line Features, Homerun Productions and Portman Entertainment Group
results.brief_description: Grace Trevethan (Brenda Blethyn) is a passionate gardener and housewife whose cloistered world is threatened by the sudden death of her husband. Grace discovers that not only was he unfaithful but he also left her saddled with enormous debt. Having no means of supporting herself, Grace is convinced by a friend to apply her considerable horticulture skills to growing marijuana and make a quick fortune. All goes well until Grace has to deal with the seedy trading of the goods and the attentions of the local constabulary. “Saving Grace”, directed by Nigel Cole, can probably best be described as quirky feel-good English comedy with a touch of dark humour nudging the sides; it also has undertones of female liberation as Grace gains empowerment over her life. Brenda Blethyn is credible as Grace but the comedy flounders often as, despite some enjoyably anarchic moments, the narrative also calls for some dreadfully unlikely ones. Cast also includes: Craig Ferguson, Martin Clunes, Tcheky Karyo, and Jamie Forman.

results.title: The Last time I saw Paris
results.creator_credit: Richard Brooks, Jack Cummings and Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer
results.brief_description: Charles Wills (Van Johnson) is a GI with literary ambitions who goes to Paris and meets and falls in love with the wealthy Helen Ellswirth (Elizabeth Taylor). Charles’ ambition to write a novel falls to pieces as failure to sell his writing causes him to turn to alcohol. During a drunken argument Johnson accidentally locks Helen out of their Parisian apartment, causing her to catch a fatal dose of pneumonia. When Helen dies, custody of their child is granted to Helen’s sister, who always disapproved of her sister’s abusive partner. Shocked into action, Charles returns home and finally finds success as a writer, and the conviction to try and regain custody of his daughter. Based on the tragic love story by F. Scott Fitzgerald. Cast also includes Donna Reed, Walter Pidgeon and Eva Gabor.

results.title: Nothing sacred
results.creator_credit: William Wellman, David O. Selznick and Selznick International Pictures
results.brief_description: Carol Lombard is hilariously funny playing the role of Hazel Flagg in this outstanding black comedy. Hazel’s bumbling doctor mistakenly diagnoses her as having fatal radium poisoning. Although she’s never felt better, Hazel pretends to be dying in order to accept the offer of an all-expenses-paid visit to New York City made by a newspaper reporter. By the time the reporter begins churning out daily reports of the tragedy, half of Manhattan is in tears and Hazel is built up to be a brave ‘suffering in silence’ charmer and is lionized by the media and public. When Hazel cannot disguise her good health any longer, a group of her financial backers wise up to her ruse and ask her to find a way to die and save them all further embarassment.This was one of the first screwball comedies ever produced, and possibly one of the finest.

results.title: Dangerous game
results.creator_credit: Stephen Hopkins, Basil Appleby, Judith West, Quantum Films and Virgo Productions
results.brief_description: Dangerous game is director Stephen Hopkins’ debut feature film but he is better known for his later features “Nightmare on Elm Street 5”, “Predator 2”, “Lost in space”, “Blown away” and several TV productions, including “24”. This Australian thriller, set in Sydney, centres around a group of five uni students, David (Miles Buchanan), Jack (Marcus Graham), Tony (John Polson), Kathryn (Kathryn Walker) and Ziggy (Sandie Lillingston) whose lives are suddenly turned upside down when they decide on a whim, late one night, to break into a department store - just for the sheer thrill of it all. What they were unprepared for, however, was the presence of Murphy (Steven Grives) the psycho cop who had, for reasons of his own, taken to stalking and harrassing Jack, pursuing him in a menacing, almost fanatical, manner to such a point that he lost his job that very day through Jack reporting him. Now Murphy is the security guard in the department store and he wants revenge. From a fairly slow start, the film gradually builds up into an action thriller with some tense moments and unexpected outcomes. Based on an original script by Michael Ralph, screenplay by Peter West and cinematography by Peter Levy.

results.title: Unknown pleasures = Ren Xiao yao [DVD]
results.creator_credit: Jia Zhang-ke, Li Kit-ming, Shozo Ichiyama, Bitters End (Unknown pleasures)--Beijing Film Academy, E-Pictures, Hu Tong Communications, Lumen Films, Office Kitano, Radiant Advertising Company and T-Mark
results.brief_description: Unknown pleasures: Set in the provincial Chinese city of Datong in 2001, the story follows the lives of two 19 year-old unemployed and disenchanted friends Bin Bin (Zhao Wei Wei) and Xiao Ji (Wu Qiong) who spend their days aimlessly wandering around dreaming of ways of escaping their meaningless and boring existences. China, with its rapidly changing political and economical role in the world, finds itself being drawn into the web of globalisation and the World Trade Organisation and televisions all over China are blasting out the message of change to the Chinese population. However, for the alienated Bin Bin and Xiao Ji, it is the day-to-day survival that counts. Poignant and slow-moving. Cast also includes Zhao Tao, Zhou Qing Feng. “Xiao Wu”: Jia Zhang-Ke’s directorial debut low-budget film shot on 16mm with non-professional actors about a small time thief and pickpocket, Xiao Wu, operating in a small provincial town at a time of immense social change in China. Xiao Wu finds it difficult to adapt to the new China and worse still, that he is becoming an outcast facing rejection from a potential relationship and even his own parents. A tragi-comedy. Cast includes Wang Hong Wei, Hao Hong Jian, Zuo Bai Tao, Ma Jin Rei, Liu Jun Ying. Director of photography for both these beautifully directed films is Yu Lik Wai.

results.title: L.I.E. = LIE [DVD]
results.creator_credit: Michael Cuesta, Linda Moran, Rene Bastian, Alter Ego and Belladonna
results.brief_description: Writer/director Michael Cuesta’s feature film debut deals with the difficult, often emotive, subject of paedophilia with compassion and sensitivity combined with an air of realism and awareness of teenage sexuality and the complexities of life that often face young male adolescents as they forge their way into adulthood. L.I.E., which stands for Long Island Expressway, is a symbolic reminder to 15 year-old Howie (Paul Franklin Dano) who has recently lost his mother through a car accident on the L.I.E. and is struggling to cope emotionally with her loss. His father Marty (Bruce Altman) who finds solace in a shallow based-on-sex relationship with his new girlfriend is also on the run from the law through his corrupt business practices and barely has time for his lonely grieving son. Disillusioned and confused, Howie forms a bond with three rather rebellious and streetwise dysfunctionals who spend more time out of school than in, robbing local houses to finance their habits. The relationship between Howie, and Gary (Billy Kay), who unbeknownst to Howie sometimes works as a male prostitute, evolves into something beyond friendship, causing Howie to reflect on his sexuality and his feelings towards Gary. But the issue of Howie’s sexuality soon takes another path when through one of his robbery escapades with Gary, he meets middle-aged ex-marine “Big John” Harrigan (Brian Cox) - a regular customer of Gary’s who has a penchant for boys in their early teens. When Gary runs off to California, and his father is arrested by the FBI, Howie finds an unexpected degree of emotional support in his relationship with the rather curious and compassionate paedophile who seemingly understands Howie’s tenuous and helpless situation. A very moving story with moments of warmth and humour but which also delves into the murkier side of urban life and paedophilia. In the director’s words “It was vital the audience could relate to Big John, even sympathise with him, but I tried very hard to make sure his intentions were constantly blurred”. Superb cinemaphotography by Romeo Tirone and outstanding performances by the lead actors, makes this multi-award winning film compelling viewing, both emotionally and psychologically. (Awards: Edinburgh 2001, Stockholm 2001, Independent Spirit Awards 2002) [End of file]

results.title: Le Revelateur
results.brief_description: Philippe Garrel’s first film was made when he was 23 as an oblique response to the political crisis of May 1968 in France, triggered by student revolt. The images are austerely symbolic representations of the older generation’s traumatic reaction to conflict. They suggest a feeling of collective psychosis. In the opening shot a young child sits on a bunk; soon a man and a woman enter the frame but fail to connect the child, leaving him alone. Throughout the narrative these three characters seek to connect but are continuously disembodied within the frame. The beautiful expressionistic black and white cinematography of this piece is enrapturing. The silent track makes the images even more haunting and oppressive. At various points the presence behind the camera is directly invoked by the actors, presumably with the intention of implicating the audience in the landscape. Cast: Bernadette Lafont, Laurent Terzieff, Stanislaus Robiol. Please note: silent film, no audio track.

The beautiful expressionistic black and white cinematography of this piece is enrapturing. The silent track makes the images even more haunting and oppressive. At various points the presence behind the camera is directly invoked by the actors, presumably with the intention of implicating the audience in the landscape. Cast: Bernadette Lafont, Laurent Terzieff, Stanislaus Robiol. Please note: silent film, no audio track.

results.title: Made in Hong Kong = Xianggang zhizao [Widescreen]
results.creator_credit: Fruit Chan, Andrew Lau, Doris Yang, Nicetop Independent and Team Work Production House
results.brief_description: This low budget teen flick, the first independent film to emerge from the former British colony, certainly captured the critics’ praise and received several international awards for its young Chinese director, Fruit Chan. The central character, Autumn Moon (Sam Lee), who, abandoned by his father and living with his mother in the back streets of urban Hong Kong where crime, gangs and violence exist on a daily basis, operates as a small-time debt collector for Wing, his triad “big brother”. Moon is a survivor and although he acts cool and mean, can also show compassion and tenderness, particularly for the under-dog as in the case of Sylvester (Wenbers Li), a mentally handicapped friend whom he zealously protects from bullying gangs and Ping (Neiky Yim), the daughter of one of his debtors, who is suffering from a fatal kidney disease and who Moon soon finds himself falling for. A young schoolgirl’s suicide will inextricably link the lives of Moon, Sylvester and Ping taking them down a sad and tragic path of despair and violence but one begins to empathise with Moon along this journey as he tries to protect those he loves. Gritty, tender, violent, comic, “Made in Hong Kong” is film noir in style with Moon acting as narrator throughout the story but it is also expressionistic visually, using strobe, overexposure, freeze-frames, and jump-cutting, adding depth and enhancement to Chan’s debut film. Cast also includes Amy Tam, Carol Lam, Doris Chow, Wu Wai-chung. In Mandarin with English subtitles. (Awards: Locarno; Gijon; Hong Kong)

results.title: Ai qing wan sui = Vive l'amour [Widescreen]
results.creator_credit: Tsai Ming-liang, Ligong Xu, Central Motion Pictures Corporation, Shiung Fa Films and Sunny Overseas
results.brief_description: A film to capture the senses “Vive L’amour” is visually stylistic and narratively minimalist, where long silences are interrupted by the sound of running water, a cassette tape being inserted into a recorder, the inhaling of a cigarette or the intense breathing of one in the act of committing suicide. It is a tale of three lost souls in the equally soul-less modern city of Taipei whose lives repeatedly and unwittingly cross paths in the same uptown luxury, albeit sterile, apartment: Hsaio-kang (Lee Kang-sheng), a gay salesman of crematorium niches coming to terms with his own homosexuality, Ah-jung (Chen Chao-jung) a street hawker of imitation designer goods and Mai Lin (Yang Kuei-mei) a struggling real estate agent. A sense of desperation lingers beneath the normality of routine daily life of each of the protagonists, drawing the three together and enmeshing their souls in a most unlikely liaison. Evocative and melancholic, “Vive L’amour” is beautifully directed by Tsai Ming Liang (The River) who is recognised as a major player in the New Taiwanese Cinema, with superb cinematography by Liao Peng-Jung and Lin Ming-Kuo. (Award: Golden Lion at the 1994 Venice Film Festival) In Mandarin with English subtitles.
